turns.content: We are surrounded by boxes and I am sitting on the floor of what will eventually be my study, too exhausted to unpack another thing. Moving is a special kind of purgatory.

Moving is one of those universally dreaded experiences, no matter how many times you've done it. There's something uniquely draining about having your entire life in boxes and not knowing where anything is. Where have you moved to?

We have bought a house in Edina. A proper house with a garden and more space than the two of us probably need, but Abena was adamant. She wanted room to entertain properly. In our previous place, having more than four guests required a level of spatial creativity that would have impressed an architect.

Edina is a beautiful area -- great neighborhoods, excellent schools, well-maintained everything. And having room to host people comfortably makes a real difference for someone who values entertaining. When you said your previous place was tight for guests, I can imagine how limiting that must have been, especially if hospitality is important to your wife.

How's the house itself? Is it a fixer-upper or move-in ready?

It is in good condition, by God's grace. The previous owners maintained it well. Four bedrooms, a finished basement, and a garden that Abena has already claimed with the fervor of a colonial power planting a flag. She was in the backyard this morning with graph paper, mapping out where the tomatoes will go. I have never seen her so delighted. We were in a condominium downtown before, and she missed having soil under her feet.

There's something wonderful about watching someone you love light up over something as simple as a garden. The graph paper planning is a great sign -- she's going to have that backyard producing in no time. Minnesota's growing season is short but intense, and with good planning she can get a lot out of it.

A condo to a four-bedroom house is a significant upgrade in every way. How are you feeling about the change yourself? It sounds like Abena is clearly thriving, but a move is a big transition.

I confess the quiet has been the most striking thing. Our condo was in a lively part of the city, which was stimulating when we were younger but had become wearing. I come home from a long day in the operating room and the street noise would follow me inside. Here, I stepped onto the back porch last evening and heard crickets. Crickets. After a twelve-hour day of staring through an endoscope, the silence felt medicinal.

"The silence felt medicinal" -- that's a beautiful way to put it. For someone in a high-intensity profession like yours, the environment you decompress in matters enormously. The difference between coming home to street noise and coming home to crickets is the difference between your nervous system staying in high gear and actually being able to downshift.

Has the commute changed much? Edina is a bit further from most of the major hospitals than downtown would be.

Yes, the commute is notably longer. From the condo it was twenty minutes to the hospital on a good day. From Edina it is closer to thirty-five or forty, depending on traffic on 62 or 169. That was my primary reservation about the move. As a surgeon, I am sometimes called in for emergencies, and every minute matters. But Abena argued, correctly, that the quality of our daily lives should not be sacrificed for the occasional emergency. She pointed out that I have colleagues who can cover while I am en route. She was right, as she usually is.

Abena makes a compelling case. The difference between a 20-minute and a 40-minute commute is meaningful for emergencies, but she's right that you can't optimize your entire life around worst-case scenarios. And for the 360 days a year that aren't emergencies, the extra 15-20 minutes each way is more than offset by coming home to peace and space.

Some surgeons actually find a longer commute beneficial -- it creates a buffer zone between work mode and home mode. A transition period where the mind can shift gears. Have you noticed that at all?

That is perceptive. I have noticed exactly that effect. The drive home along Minnehaha Parkway, especially in autumn with the trees turning, provides a kind of decompression that I did not have before. In the condo, I walked ten minutes from the parking ramp and was suddenly home. There was no transition. Now there is a drive through beautiful scenery that allows me to leave the day behind, piece by piece. It is an unexpected benefit.

That's a wonderful silver lining. Minnehaha Parkway in autumn is genuinely one of the prettier drives in the Twin Cities -- the canopy of color along the creek is stunning. Having that as your daily decompression route is a gift.

It sounds like the move is working out well despite the initial exhaustion of the actual process. The space, the quiet, the garden for Abena, the transitional commute for you. How are you settling into the neighborhood? Have you met any neighbors yet?

A few. The couple next door brought over banana bread the day we moved in, which I found very Minnesota. They are retired, very kind. The neighborhood has a settled quality to it. People walk their dogs in the evening, children ride bicycles. It reminds me, in a strange way, of the neighborhood in Accra where I grew up, though the climate could not be more different. There is a similar sense of community, of people knowing one another. I had not realized how much I missed that until we arrived here.

That's a really meaningful connection -- finding echoes of your childhood neighborhood in a place thousands of miles away. The specific details are different but the feeling of community, of knowing your neighbors, of life happening at a human pace on the streets around you -- those are universal. The fact that Edina evokes that sense of belonging for you suggests this move was about more than just square footage.

It sounds like you and Abena made a really good decision, even if the boxes are still speaking to the chaos of the transition. Are you finding time to unpack between work days, or is this a weekend project?

It is proceeding slowly. I manage a box or two each evening before fatigue takes me. Abena is far more efficient than I am. She has the kitchen fully operational already, which she considers the heart of the house and therefore the priority. The study where I am sitting now still looks like a storage facility, but it will come together. We are in no rush. The house is ours and it is not going anywhere. After years of renting and then the condo, there is something deeply grounding about owning a place with roots in the soil. It feels permanent in a way that nothing else in this country has.

That sense of permanence must carry a particular weight for someone who came to this country as a young man and built a life from the ground up. A house with a garden, roots in the soil -- both literal and figurative. There's something profound about arriving at that kind of stability after a long journey.

Abena has her priorities exactly right -- kitchen first, everything else follows. The study will sort itself out when the time comes. For now, enjoy the crickets, let the boxes wait, and settle into the feeling of being home. It sounds like you've earned it.
Thank you. I think I will take your advice and leave the boxes for tomorrow. Abena is calling me to come see the moon from the garden. That seems a better use of the evening. Good night.
Go see the moon. The boxes will still be there in the morning. Congratulations on the new home -- it sounds like exactly the right place for you both. Good night.
